name: Holy See (Vatican City)

visa_free.nationality: All nationalities (de facto — no separate Vatican visa system exists; entry is governed by Italy/Schengen rules under the Lateran Treaty open-border arrangement)
visa_free.notes: Vatican City has no border controls with Italy per the Lateran Treaty of 11 February 1929. The five entrances into Vatican City State are manned by the Pontifical Swiss Guard and Gendarmerie Corps but entry formalities for foreign visitors are governed by Italy/Schengen rules, not a separate Vatican visa. The governing legislation is Law N. CXXXI of 22 February 2011 on citizenship, residence and access (which replaced the original Law N. III of 7 June 1929). Chapter Three of Law N. CXXXI concerns the authorisation non-citizens and non-residents need in order to access Vatican City State; however, the full text is not publicly available in English online. In practice, no separate Vatican visa is required; visitors who can lawfully enter Italy under Schengen rules can enter Vatican City. Vatican authorities have not published a nationality-specific visa-free list. Note: ETIAS (European Travel Information and Authorisation System) is expected to be implemented by the EU in late 2026, at which point nationals of approximately 59 currently visa-exempt countries (including the US, UK, Canada, Australia, Japan, South Korea) will require an ETIAS authorisation before entering Italy/Schengen and by extension Vatican City. ETIAS is a travel authorisation, not a visa, and does not change Vatican City's underlying policy of having no separate national visa system.

visa_policy.notes: Vatican City does not operate a standalone national visa system. The open-border arrangement with Italy under the Lateran Treaty (1929) means entry to Vatican City is contingent on lawful entry into Italy/Schengen. No official Vatican government page enumerates per-nationality visa-free, VOA, or e-visa lists. The official vaticanstate.va population page confirms that access formalities are set by provisions aligned with the Lateran Treaty. The governing law is Law N. CXXXI of 22 February 2011 on citizenship, residence and access (replaced the original Law N. III of 7 June 1929). ETIAS — the EU-level travel authorisation expected in late 2026 — will apply de facto to Vatican City visitors from approximately 59 currently visa-exempt countries, as they must first enter Italy to reach Vatican City.

cbi.notes: No citizenship-by-investment or golden visa program exists. Vatican citizenship is granted solely by the Holy See to individuals holding qualifying ecclesiastical or administrative appointments under Law N. CXXXI of 22 February 2011. Citizenship is functional and temporary in nature (automatic for Cardinals, Holy See diplomats, Swiss Guards, and persons residing in Vatican City by reason of office or service), and is lost automatically when qualifying employment or residence ends. Only the Pope holds Vatican citizenship for life. Confirmed absent from all major CBI/golden-visa program lists and consistent with official Vatican sources.

agreements: Lateran Treaty open-border arrangement with Italy (1929) — no border controls between Vatican City and Italy

validation_notes: Law citation corrected: the original governing law was Law N. III of 7 June 1929; it was replaced by Law N. CXXXI of 22 February 2011 on citizenship, residence and access. Both references are now accurately reflected in the notes.

Added ETIAS context: ETIAS (EU travel authorisation) is expected to be implemented in late 2026 and will apply de facto to Vatican City visitors from approximately 59 currently visa-exempt countries who must enter Italy first.
cbi notes updated to reference Law N. CXXXI of 2011 explicitly and to accurately describe the Pope as the only Vatican citizen for life.
Geography page verified: confirmed five entrances protected by Pontifical Swiss Guard and Gendarmerie Corps.
vaticanstate.va population page re-verified: confirms access formalities regulated by Lateran Treaty-aligned provisions. No new nationality-specific information found.
No change to conditional_access (remains empty): Vatican City publishes no bilateral credential-based or diplomatic-passport-only entry rules on any official domain.
gaps: Full text of Law N. CXXXI of 22 February 2011 on citizenship, residence and access (Chapter Three: authorisation for non-citizens/non-residents to access Vatican City) is not publicly available in English online; Vatican authorities have not published a nationality-by-nationality visa policy document.

No official English-language page from Vatican authorities enumerates specific visa-free nationalities, visa-on-arrival, or e-visa categories — only non-official aggregators cover this, so no per-nationality entries were recorded.
Vatican City's de facto Schengen-adjacent status is widely reported but not formally documented on an official Vatican government page in accessible English.

No official Vatican government source (vaticanstate.va, vatican.va, epass.vatican.va) publishes conditional entry rules based on holding third-country visas (e.g. US, Schengen, UK), residence permits, or OCI cards. Because Vatican entry is governed by Italy/Schengen rules under the Lateran Treaty, such conditional rules (if any) would be found in Italian/Schengen law, not Vatican law.

No bilateral visa-suppression agreements for diplomatic/service passport holders were found on any official Vatican domain. Wikipedia cites a 2017 Vatican–Russia visa-free diplomatic travel agreement, but the agreement text was not found on any official Vatican page; not recorded per official-source-first rules.
epass.vatican.va PDF documents (Dossier_INFO_ENGL.pdf, VAT_passports_gen_info.pdf) could not be parsed; may contain additional information about passport-specific agreements.
ETIAS implementation timeline (late 2026) sourced from non-official aggregators; official ETIAS launch date subject to change by EU authorities.

outbound_official.note: no official Holy See (Vatican City) government outbound visa-list page located; rely on destination-side data. The Holy See's official domains (vatican.va, vaticanstate.va, epass.vatican.va) publish no page enumerating where Vatican ordinary-passport citizens may travel without a prior consular visa. epass.vatican.va covers only technical passport specifications. The Secretariat of State pages cover diplomatic relations and institutional matters, not citizen travel guidance. With only ~673 Vatican citizens — most of them clergy or Swiss Guards in diplomatic roles — outbound travel guidance is handled through diplomatic channels rather than a public webpage.
